original_abstract:
This study investigates the behavior of nitrogen centers acting as hydrogen-bond or Brønsted acceptors within solid molecular acid-base complexes. By utilizing N 1s X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and $^{15}$N solid-state nuclear magnetic resonance (ssNMR) spectroscopy, we correlate electronic and magnetic properties with local crystallographic environments determined by X-ray diffraction (XRD). We demonstrate that the substantial shift in N 1s binding energy following nitrogen protonation provides a definitive diagnostic tool for distinguishing between protonated salts and neutral co-crystals. These findings are corroborated by significant low-frequency ssNMR shifts observed upon proton transfer from the acid to the base. In contrast, co-crystal formation typically induces only minor chemical shifts, except in cases involving exceptionally strong hydrogen bonds. 

Density functional theory (DFT) calculations using CASTEP show a strong correlation with experimental $^{15}$N ssNMR isotropic shifts. This confirms that computational modeling of hydrogen-bond strengths and associated NMR parameters can reliably differentiate between salt and co-crystal architectures (NMR crystallography). The high degree of consistency between XPS data and combined CASTEP/ssNMR analyses establishes a robust framework for investigating local structures in molecular systems where long-range order is absent, such as non-crystalline or amorphous materials. Probing 17 distinct systems, this research highlights the versatility of this multi-spectroscopic approach for a wide range of applications across organic, physical, and materials chemistry.
humanized_abstract:
Nitrogen centres acting as hydrogen bond or Brønsted acid acceptors in solid molecular acidbase complexes have been investigated using N 1s X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) in conjunction with $^{15}$N solid-state nuclear magnetic resonance (ssNMR) spectroscopy together with X-ray diffraction (XRD) to correlate electronic and magnetic centres with their sites within the solid state. We show that large changes in binding energy following protonation together with the fact that salts and neutral co-crystals can give rise to very different XPS spectra signify that the changes in the N 1s binding energy of nitrogen centres in acidbase complexes can serve as a sensitive diagnostic technique for the assessment of the nature of nitrogen centres in protonated salts and in neutral co-crystals of acids and bases. This is substantiated by large low frequency ssNMR shifts occurring following protonation of the base by the acid whilst changes in the NMR spectrum of the co-crystal are normally small except for very strong hydrogen-bonded species.

By employing CASTEP-DFT modelling we show that density functional theory strongly correlates with experimental $^{15}$N ssNMR isotropic shifts, enabling modeling of the effects of the hydrogen-bonding interactions on the NMR parameters which can be used to differentiate between a salt versus a co-crystal form of a compound, demonstrating NMR crystallography. Our XPS results correlate very strongly with the results obtained from modelling and $^{15}$N ssNMR that demonstrates that the approach is valid and that the information provided by the technique(s) can be used to elucidate the local structure at the surface of materials or in non-crystalline regimes such as in amorphous systems. Overall, 17 systems were studied demonstrating wide scope of applications of multi-spectroscopic approach across organic, physical and materials chemistry.

document.output:
Nitrogen centres acting as hydrogen bond or Brønsted acid acceptors in solid molecular acidbase complexes have been investigated using N 1s X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) in conjunction with $^{15}$N solid-state nuclear magnetic resonance (ssNMR) spectroscopy together with X-ray diffraction (XRD) to correlate electronic and magnetic centres with their sites within the solid state. This is substantiated by large low frequency ssNMR shifts occurring following protonation of the base by the acid whilst changes in the NMR spectrum of the co-crystal are normally small except for very strong hydrogen-bonded species.

By employing CASTEP-DFT modelling we show that density functional theory strongly correlates with experimental $^{15}$N ssNMR isotropic shifts, enabling modeling of the effects of the hydrogen-bonding interactions on the NMR parameters which can be used to differentiate between a salt versus a co-crystal form of a compound, demonstrating NMR crystallography. document.input:
This study investigates the behavior of nitrogen centers acting as hydrogen-bond or Brønsted acceptors within solid molecular acid-base complexes.
